follow_ups.question: How has Instagram been as a platform for sharing your art?
follow_ups.response: It's been really positive, actually. I've connected with other generative artists and gotten invited to a couple of online group shows through contacts I made there. The algorithm can be frustrating -- some posts get way more reach than others for no obvious reason -- but it's the best platform I've found for visual work. I've had a few people reach out about buying prints, which is flattering even though I'm not set up to sell yet. I also use it to document my creative process, like time-lapse videos of paintings. That content does surprisingly well.

follow_ups.question: How do you manage the mental health aspect of being on multiple platforms?
follow_ups.response: It's a conscious effort. I went through a phase where I was doom-scrolling on Twitter for hours and it was terrible for my mental state. Now I use app timers -- 20 minutes max per platform per session. I also curate my feeds aggressively. If an account makes me feel bad about myself or angry without being informative, I unfollow. I've muted a lot of political accounts and keywords on Twitter. And the 10 PM cutoff is about not going to bed with my brain full of other people's content. I want my last hour of the day to be painting or reading or just quiet. It doesn't always work, but having the intention helps.

transcript.question_text: Some people say they use Facebook or Twitter to get (emotional or financial) support during tough times. What about you?
transcript.response: Not really for financial support, no. But emotional support, in a way, yes. The Discord server for Colombian scientists has been a place where I've vented about career frustrations and gotten genuine, empathetic responses. When my paper got rejected, I posted about it there and like eight people responded with encouragement and their own rejection stories. That felt like real support. I've also DM'd friends through Instagram when I'm having a rough day. But I wouldn't post publicly about a tough time on Facebook or Twitter -- that doesn't feel safe to me. The support I get online is through smaller, more private communities where I trust the people.

transcript.question_text: Tell me about any recent changes in your level of stress, worry, and your emotional coping strategies.
transcript.response: The UC Davis collaboration has added a new layer of stress because there are more moving parts and more people depending on me to deliver on time. I've been feeling that pressure. But I've also gotten better at coping. I'm more consistent with yoga than I was six months ago. I've started doing a five-minute breathing exercise in the morning before I check my phone -- just sitting with my coffee and breathing. Small thing, but it starts the day calmer. And I've gotten better at saying no to things. I used to overcommit -- every hackathon, every event, every volunteer opportunity. Now I'm more selective. I'd rather do fewer things well than spread myself thin. That's been a real shift in the last few months.

transcript.question_text: This might be repetitive, but before we go on to the next section, I want to quickly make sure I have this right: who are you living with right now?
transcript.response: Just me and my cat, Luna. One-bedroom apartment off Mangrove Avenue in Chico. No one else.

transcript.question_text: Any babies or small children?
transcript.response: No, no children.

transcript.question_text: Anyone who usually lives here but is away -- traveling, at school, or in a hospital?
transcript.response: Nope, nobody.

transcript.question_text: Any lodgers, boarders, or employees who live here?
transcript.response: No, just me.

transcript.question_text: Who shares the responsibility for the rent, mortgage, or other household expenses?
transcript.response: It's all me. I cover rent, utilities, everything.

transcript.question_text: Now we'd like to talk about how you make ends meet and what the monthly budget looks like for you and your family. What were your biggest expenses last month?
transcript.response: Rent is always the big one -- $1,250 a month for my one-bedroom. That's actually pretty reasonable for California, which is one reason I stay in Chico. After that, groceries were probably the next biggest. I spent more than usual last month because I stocked up at the farmers market and bought some specialty ingredients for a recipe I was trying. Maybe $350 on food total. Then car insurance, gas, utilities -- those are all in the $50 to $150 range. And I had to pay for a new set of tires, which was about $400. That one hurt.

follow_ups.question: Do you have a formal budget you follow?
follow_ups.response: Sort of. I have a spreadsheet where I track my spending by category. I update it every week or two. It's not super rigid -- I don't have hard limits for each category -- but it lets me see where the money's going and catch anything unusual. I set it up after the divorce when I was suddenly managing everything alone. Before that, my ex handled most of the finances, so I had to learn fast. The spreadsheet has been a lifeline.

transcript.question_text: How much did your household spend in the past month? Is that the usual amount? And if not, how much do you usually spend and on what?
transcript.response: Last month was higher than usual because of the tires. Total was probably around $2,800 to $3,000. A normal month is more like $2,200 to $2,500. That covers rent, utilities, groceries, gas, insurance, phone, internet, and incidentals. I also set aside money for Luna's food and vet expenses -- about $60 a month. And there's my coffee habit, which I don't even want to calculate. I probably spend $40 a month on beans alone, plus the occasional coffee shop visit.

transcript.question_text: Does your household own or rent your home?
transcript.response: I rent. It's a one-bedroom apartment in a small complex, maybe 12 units. The landlord is a local family, not a big management company, which I appreciate. They're responsive when something needs fixing and they haven't raised my rent aggressively. I've been here about two years now. Owning is a dream but not realistic right now. Housing prices in California, even in Chico, are tough. I've looked at the market casually and I'd need a significant down payment that I don't have yet. Maybe in a few years.

transcript.question_text: Bills can fluctuate over the course of a year. Seasons change, there are holidays and special events, school starts and ends, and so on. Tell me all about how your bills have fluctuated over the past year.
transcript.response: The biggest fluctuation is utilities. Summer electricity bills are brutal because of the AC -- Chico gets over 100 degrees regularly and my apartment doesn't have great insulation. In July and August, my PG&E bill can hit $180 or more, versus maybe $60 in the winter. Holiday season bumps up my spending because I buy gifts for family -- I budget about $300 to $400 for Christmas. Travel costs spike when there's a conference, but my lab covers registration fees and sometimes partial travel. The other thing is the wildfire smoke season -- I stock up on air filters, which are like $30 each, and I go through several during a bad season. Spring has the allergy medication bump, which is small but consistent. Nothing else is too dramatic.

transcript.question_text: Tell me all about how you coped with any extra expenses in the past months.
transcript.response: The tires last month -- I just pulled from my savings, which I try not to do but that's what it's there for. I also sold a couple of prints of my generative art pieces online, which brought in about $150, so that helped offset it. When I know a big expense is coming, like a conference trip, I'll cut back on eating out and non-essential purchases for a few weeks ahead of time. I don't use credit cards for anything I can't pay off that month. That's a rule I've set for myself. If I can't afford it outright, I wait.

transcript.question_text: Some people have a savings account, some people save in a different way, and some people say they don't save. How about you?
transcript.response: I have a savings account at a local credit union. I try to put away about $300 to $400 a month, though some months it's less if something comes up. I also contribute to a retirement account through my employer -- they match up to 3 percent, so I put in at least that. I'm not aggressive about investing. I've thought about opening a brokerage account and putting money in index funds, but I haven't gotten around to it. My savings philosophy is basically: have a cushion for emergencies, contribute to retirement, and save toward bigger goals when I can. I'm not where I want to be yet, but I'm consistent about it.

follow_ups.question: How did you develop your approach to saving?
follow_ups.response: Mostly from watching my parents. They were always very careful with money -- not fearful, but disciplined. My mom kept an envelope system for years, like literally cash in labeled envelopes. I internalized that sense of, you don't spend what you don't have. The research salary isn't huge, so I have to be intentional. The divorce was also a financial wake-up call because splitting a household is expensive. I had to furnish my apartment, set up utilities, all on one income. That forced me to get serious about tracking and saving.

transcript.question_text: Some people save for big things, like a home, while others save for a rainy day. How about for you (in the last year)?
transcript.response: Both, really. I have a general emergency fund that I try to keep at about three months of expenses. I'm not quite there -- I'm at about two and a half months right now. After the tires, I need to build it back up. Beyond that, I'm loosely saving toward a down payment for a house, but it's a long-term thing. I'm not going to be ready to buy anytime soon. And I'm also trying to save for a trip to Colombia -- I want to visit my abuela before she gets much older. That's probably $2,000 to $2,500 for a couple weeks. I'm hoping to do that later this year or early next year.

follow_ups.question: When was the last time you visited Colombia?
follow_ups.response: About three years ago. My parents, Diego, and I all went together for two weeks. We stayed with my abuela and visited Bogota, Cartagena, and my mom's hometown. It was incredible -- the food, the music, the colors. My abuela cried when she saw us. She's 82 now and getting around is harder for her, so each visit feels more urgent. I want to go at least once more before... well, while I still can.

transcript.question_text: Some people have student loans or credit card debt. Others take out loans from family or friends or find other ways of borrowing money. Tell me about all the debts you're paying on right now.
transcript.response: I have student loans from my bachelor's degree. The balance is about $18,000. I pay $220 a month on that. It's manageable but it'll be hanging over me for a while -- I'm on a standard 10-year repayment plan and I'm about four years in. No credit card debt. I use one credit card for everyday purchases but I pay it off in full every month. No car payment -- my car is a 2017 Honda Civic that I bought used and paid off already. No other debts. I've never borrowed from family, and I hope I never have to.

transcript.question_text: Tell me about any time during the past year that you haven't had enough money to buy something that you needed or pay a bill that was due.
transcript.response: Nothing critical, thankfully. There was a moment last summer where I wanted to upgrade my computer for a research project -- the processing power I needed was significant -- and the machine I wanted was about $2,500. I couldn't swing it out of pocket. I ended up getting the lab to cover part of it as a research expense, and I put the rest on my credit card and paid it off over two months, which isn't ideal but it worked. There was also a dental cleaning I put off for a few months because my copay felt like too much during a tight month. I eventually went, but the delay bothered me. Those are the kinds of compromises I make -- nothing dire, but I wish I had more of a buffer.

follow_ups.question: How did you feel about having to defer that dental appointment?
follow_ups.response: Frustrated, mostly with myself. It was only like $75 out of pocket, but that month I'd had the conference travel expenses and a car repair, and $75 felt like the straw on the camel's back. I knew rationally that skipping a dental cleaning is penny-wise and pound-foolish. But in the moment, it's about cash flow. That experience motivated me to build the emergency fund more seriously. I never want to be in a position where I'm choosing between basic healthcare and other bills.

transcript.question_text: What is or was your occupation? In this occupation, what kind of work do you do and what are the most important activities or duties?
transcript.response: I'm a computer and information research scientist. In plain language, I design and build algorithms -- mostly in the area of machine learning and natural language processing. My day-to-day involves writing code, primarily in Python and R, running experiments on large datasets, analyzing results, and writing up findings for publication. Right now, my main project is developing generative models that can create visual narratives from unstructured data. Think of it like teaching a computer to take a messy pile of information and turn it into something coherent and visually meaningful. I also do a fair amount of statistical modeling and data visualization -- taking complex results and making them understandable. A big part of the job that people don't see is writing grant proposals. Securing funding is essential for keeping the lab running, and I spend probably 15 to 20 percent of my time on that. I write papers and present at conferences, which means public speaking a few times a year. And I collaborate with other researchers, both locally and remotely. The UC Davis project I mentioned is a great example. It's a job that requires deep focus and long stretches of uninterrupted thinking, which suits my personality well. But it also requires communication and teamwork, which I've grown into over time.

follow_ups.question: What's the most rewarding part of the job for you?
follow_ups.response: That moment when an experiment works and you see something genuinely new. Like when a model produces an output you didn't expect and it's actually better than what you designed for. That's a rush. And publishing a paper -- seeing your name on something that adds to human knowledge, even in a small way. The other thing is when I can blend the art and the science. My generative visual narrative work is literally the intersection of everything I love. When I get to present that at a conference and see people's eyes light up -- that's the best feeling.

follow_ups.question: What's the most challenging part?
follow_ups.response: The uncertainty. In research, most things don't work on the first try, or the second, or the tenth. You can spend months on an approach and have it go nowhere. That requires a kind of resilience that I'm still building. The other challenge is the publish-or-perish pressure. Your value is measured in publications and citations, and that creates a constant background anxiety. And then there's the funding cycle -- grants are competitive and the process is slow. You write a proposal, wait six months, and then maybe get rejected and have to start over. It can feel like you're always auditioning for the right to keep doing your job.

transcript.question_text: Was your occupation covered by a union or employee association contract?
transcript.response: Not directly. Research scientists at my lab aren't unionized. The university's faculty union covers teaching staff, but my position is classified differently -- I'm on the research side, technically a staff position rather than faculty. There have been conversations about whether research staff should have more formal representation, but nothing has come of it yet. I'd probably support it if it happened. The main thing I'd want is more job security. Research positions can be tied to grant funding, which means your job exists as long as the grants do.

transcript.question_text: In the past year, how many weeks did you work (for a few hours, including paid vacation, paid sick leave, and military service)?
transcript.response: Pretty much all 52 weeks. I took about a week off around Christmas and a few scattered days here and there, but even on 'vacation' I'm usually checking email or reading papers. If you mean weeks where I formally worked at least some hours, it's probably 50 or 51.

transcript.question_text: How many hours each week did you usually work?
transcript.response: On a normal week, about 45 hours. During deadline weeks, it can push to 55 or 60.

transcript.question_text: In the past week, did you work for pay (even for 1 hour) at a job or business?
transcript.response: Yes, it was a regular work week for me.

transcript.question_text: Did you have more than one job or business, including part time, evening, or weekend work?
transcript.response: No, just the one research position. The volunteering and mentoring are unpaid. I've made a little money selling art prints, but I wouldn't call that a job or a business -- it's more of an occasional thing.

transcript.question_text: Now, in the past month, how many jobs did you work, including part time, evening, or weekend work?
transcript.response: Just the one.

transcript.question_text: For your job or occupations, how much money did you receive in the past month?
transcript.response: My take-home after taxes and deductions is about $4,200 a month. My gross salary works out to about $5,800 monthly. The difference goes to taxes, health insurance, and retirement contributions. It's not a lot for California, but Chico's cost of living makes it workable.

transcript.question_text: How often do you get paid?
transcript.response: Twice a month -- the 1st and the 15th.

transcript.question_text: In total, how much did your household make in the past month?
transcript.response: Since it's just me, it's the same -- about $4,200 take-home. Plus that $150 I made from the art prints, but that's not regular income. So call it $4,350 for last month specifically, but normally $4,200.

transcript.question_text: Switching gears a bit, let's talk a little about tax time. Did you file taxes last year (the most recent year)?
transcript.response: Yes, I filed. I use TurboTax -- I've been doing my own taxes since college. Last year was a little more complicated because of the divorce finalization, but it was my first year filing as single, which actually simplified things in some ways. I got a small refund, maybe $600. I always file on time. April deadlines stress me out, but I'd rather deal with it early than have it hanging over me.

transcript.question_text: Tell me more about your workplace. How long have you been at your current job? How would you describe the benefits that come with your job? (This could include things like health benefits, paid time off, vacation, and sick time.)
transcript.response: I've been at the lab for about five years now, since right after graduation. Benefits include health insurance, which I mentioned -- it's decent, covers most things with copays. I get 15 days of PTO plus sick leave and the university holidays. The retirement match at 3 percent is nice. No dental or vision separately -- that's bundled into the health plan. It's a solid package, not extravagant, but adequate.

transcript.question_text: How would you describe your relationships at work? (How is your relationship with your manager or boss? How are your relationships with your coworkers?)
transcript.response: Great, honestly. Dr. Patel, my supervisor, gives me a lot of autonomy and respects my judgment. She's tough when it comes to rigor but always fair. Kai and I collaborate well -- we have complementary skills, and he's good at the stuff I'm weaker at, like systems engineering. The grad students rotate, but I enjoy mentoring them. It's a small, collegial environment. I feel lucky.

transcript.question_text: Tell me about how predictable your work schedule is. How would you describe your job in terms of flexibility with your hours?
transcript.response: Pretty flexible, which is one of the best things about the position. There's no rigid start and end time -- as long as I'm present for meetings and making progress on my projects, nobody cares if I come in at 8 or 9:30. If I need to leave early for a yoga class or the tutoring, that's fine. The unpredictable part is the deadline crunch. When a paper submission or grant proposal is due, the schedule goes out the window and I'm working nights and weekends. Those periods are intense but they're not constant -- maybe four to six weeks a year total. The UC Davis collaboration has added more structure because I have standing meetings I have to attend, but even those are on Zoom so I can take them from anywhere. Overall, the flexibility is a huge perk and one of the reasons I've stayed despite lower pay than industry.

follow_ups.question: How important is that flexibility to your quality of life?
follow_ups.response: Essential. If I had to be at a desk from 9 to 5 with no flexibility, I think I'd burn out fast. My creativity doesn't run on a clock. Some of my best ideas come at 10 PM or on a Saturday morning hike. Having the freedom to structure my time around my energy levels rather than arbitrary hours makes me more productive, not less. It's also what allows me to do the mentoring and tutoring -- without flex hours, those wouldn't be possible.

transcript.question_text: Some people say it's hard to take or keep a job because of child care. How about for you?
transcript.response: That's not an issue for me since I don't have children.

transcript.question_text: Do you receive any payments or benefits from SNAP, food stamps, housing voucher payments, supplemental security income, or any other programs like that?
transcript.response: No, I don't receive any government assistance. My income is enough to cover my needs, even if there's not a ton left over. My parents don't receive any either, though I think they might qualify for some things they're not taking advantage of. That's something I should look into for them.

transcript.question_text: Tell me about times over the last year when your income was especially low. And tell me about all the things you did to make ends meet during that time.
transcript.response: My income is salaried, so it's pretty consistent month to month. The months that felt tight were more about unexpected expenses than low income. Like the tire replacement, or the month I had to pay for a conference flight and hotel upfront before getting reimbursed. That reimbursement lag is annoying -- you're out $800 to $1,000 for weeks. During those tight months, I cut back on eating out, skipped the coffee shop, and cooked more from basic ingredients. I also delayed any non-essential purchases. It's never been desperate, but there have been months where I was watching every dollar.

transcript.question_text: What would it be like for you if you had to spend $400 for an emergency? Would you have the money, and if not, how would you get it?
transcript.response: I could cover it from savings, though it would sting. My emergency fund is about two and a half months of expenses, so $400 is doable. I'd just transfer it from savings and then adjust my budget for the next couple months to rebuild. If it happened in the same month as another big expense -- like if the tires and an emergency both hit at once -- I'd put it on my credit card and pay it off over one to two months. But I wouldn't need to borrow from anyone or skip bills. I'm in that zone where $400 is uncomfortable but not catastrophic.

transcript.question_text: Overall, how do you feel about your financial situation?
transcript.response: Cautiously okay. I can pay my bills, I'm saving a little, I'm not in bad debt. But there's very little margin. One major unexpected expense -- like a serious car repair or a medical emergency -- would wipe out my savings. I don't have a safety net beyond what I've built myself. My parents can't help me financially; if anything, I want to be able to help them. I think about what my income would look like in industry versus research, and it's a real gap. I could probably make $40,000 to $50,000 more at a tech company. Sometimes that weighs on me, especially when I think about buying a house or starting a family someday. But then I think about the work I'm doing and the life I'm living, and I'd rather be here making less than in a cubicle in San Jose making more. I just wish research paid better. The financial stress I feel is more of a slow hum than a crisis -- it's always there but it doesn't dominate my life. I'm careful, I'm disciplined, and I'm gradually improving my position. Ask me again in five years and I think I'll be in a much better place.

follow_ups.question: What specific financial goals are you working toward right now?
follow_ups.response: Short-term: rebuild the emergency fund to a full three months, which means adding about $1,500. Medium-term: save for the Colombia trip -- I need about $2,000 more for that. Long-term: start seriously saving for a down payment. I've crunched the numbers and even in Chico, I'd need at least $40,000 to $50,000 for a modest house. That's years away. I'm also thinking about whether I should go back to school for a PhD, which would affect my finances one way or another -- might open higher-paying positions eventually but would mean lower income during the program.

transcript.question_text: Are you now married, widowed, divorced, separated, or have you never been married? If you are not currently married, are you currently living with a romantic partner?

transcript.question_text: Generally speaking, do you usually think of yourself as a Democrat, a Republican, an Independent, or what? And how strongly do you associate with that party?
transcript.response: I tend to lean toward one side but I don't carry a strong party identity. I register and vote in the primaries that feel most aligned with my values. I care more about specific policy positions -- immigration, education funding, climate, tech policy -- than about party loyalty. I've voted for candidates from different parties at the local level based on who I thought would actually do the work. I read a lot of policy analysis and I form opinions issue by issue rather than adopting a platform wholesale. If I had to put myself on a spectrum, I'm left of center, but I don't wake up thinking about party politics.

follow_ups.question: What issues would make you consider crossing party lines for a candidate?
follow_ups.response: If a candidate had a really strong, evidence-based plan for wildfire prevention and climate resilience, I'd listen regardless of party. Same with someone who had serious proposals for affordable housing in places like Chico. At the local level, competence and integrity matter more to me than party. I voted for a city council member last cycle who ran as an independent because she had the best grasp of the housing issue. But on national issues like immigration, I'm pretty firm in my leanings because the stakes are personal.

transcript.question_text: Do you think of yourself as closer to the Democratic Party or to the Republican Party?
transcript.response: Closer to the Democrats, though I have my frustrations with them too. On immigration, healthcare, education, and climate, their platform is more aligned with what I care about. But I don't feel a strong tribal attachment. I vote for them more often than not, but I wish there were more emphasis on practical problem-solving and less on messaging and culture wars from all sides. I'd love a political landscape that was more about evidence and less about identity, but I also know that's naive given how things are structured.

transcript.response: Sacramento, California. Same house my parents are still in.

transcript.question_text: Did you live with both your own mother and father when you were 16?
transcript.response: Yes, both of them. They've been together my whole life. Still are.

transcript.question_text: Who else did you live with?
transcript.response: My brother Diego. He would've been about 12 at that point. It was the four of us in a three-bedroom house. Pretty standard setup.

transcript.question_text: What's the highest degree or grade your dad completed?
transcript.response: He finished high school in Colombia. He didn't go to college. He's incredibly smart and capable, but college just wasn't in the cards for him coming from his background.

transcript.question_text: What's the highest degree or grade your mom completed?
transcript.response: She did a couple years of college in Colombia before they moved. She studied early childhood education but didn't finish the degree.

transcript.question_text: Did your mom work for pay for at least a year while you were growing up?
transcript.response: Yes, she worked most of my childhood.

transcript.question_text: What was her job? What kind of work did she do? What were her most important activities or duties?
transcript.response: She worked at a daycare center as a teaching assistant. She helped with the younger kids -- ages two to four. Her duties were helping with activities, preparing snacks, supervising playtime, and assisting the lead teacher. She was wonderful with the kids. Parents would request her by name. Her background in early childhood education from Colombia helped, even without the degree. She eventually moved up to a lead teaching assistant role. She loved the work, even though the pay wasn't great.

transcript.question_text: What kind of place did she work for? What kind of business was it? What did they make or do where she worked?
transcript.response: It was a private daycare and preschool called Little Stars, in our neighborhood in Sacramento. Small operation, maybe 40 kids total. Family-owned -- the owner was a woman named Mrs. Chen who ran it for like 30 years. They provided childcare and early learning for kids from infancy through pre-K. My mom worked there for about 12 years. When Mrs. Chen retired, the place closed, and my mom moved to part-time babysitting and housekeeping work. She still does some of that now.

transcript.question_text: Did your dad work for pay for at least a year while you were growing up?
transcript.response: Yes, absolutely. He's been working since before I was born.

transcript.question_text: What was his job? What kind of work did he do? What were his most important activities or duties?
transcript.response: He's a maintenance supervisor at a property management company. He's been there for about 20 years. He manages the upkeep of several apartment complexes and commercial buildings in the Sacramento area. His duties include coordinating repair work, doing hands-on maintenance himself -- plumbing, electrical, basic carpentry -- supervising a small team of maintenance workers, and handling emergency calls. He's the guy people call when a pipe bursts at 2 AM. He takes a lot of pride in his work. Everything he touches runs smoothly.

transcript.question_text: What kind of place did he work for? What kind of business was it? What did they make or do where he worked?
transcript.response: It's a mid-sized property management company based in Sacramento called Valley Properties. They manage residential apartment complexes and some small commercial buildings throughout the greater Sacramento area. Maybe 15 to 20 properties total. It's a family-owned company -- not a big corporation. My dad has worked his way up from a general maintenance worker to the supervisor role. He manages a team of about four people now. The owner, Mr. Torres, is also Colombian, which is how my dad got the job originally through community connections.

transcript.question_text: We all have hopes about what our future will look like. Imagine yourself a few years from now. Maybe you want your life to be the same in some ways as it is now. Maybe you want it to be different in some ways. What do you hope for?
transcript.response: A few years from now, I want to be leading my own research team. Not a huge team, but a small, interdisciplinary group that sits at the intersection of AI and creative expression. I want to have published work that genuinely advances how we think about generative models and visual storytelling. I'm thinking about doing a PhD, and if I do, I'd want to be at a place that lets me keep blending art and science rather than forcing me into a narrow lane. I'd love to have my own solo art exhibition -- something that showcases the generative work alongside traditional paintings, showing the conversation between them. I hope to have visited my abuela in Colombia again, hopefully more than once. I want my parents to be healthy and able to retire comfortably, and I want to be in a position to help with that if they need it. Personally, I'd like to be in a place where I'm open to a relationship again -- not rushing it, but not closed off either. Maybe I've met someone who gets both the scientist and the artist in me. And I hope I'm still in Chico, still hiking those trails, still going to game nights. I don't want a dramatically different life -- I want a deeper, more secure version of this one. And I really want to have started that startup I've been dreaming about, or at least be on the path. An AI-driven creative tools company that helps designers and musicians do things they couldn't do before. That's the big dream.

follow_ups.question: You mentioned the startup idea. How far along are you in thinking about that?
follow_ups.response: It's still pretty conceptual. I have a notes doc that's like 20 pages long with ideas, potential features, market analysis I've done casually. I've talked to some people in the generative art community about what tools they wish existed, and there's real demand. The gap is between having the idea and having the resources -- capital, a co-founder, business knowledge. I'm a researcher, not an entrepreneur, so I'd need to partner with someone who has that skill set. I think the PhD might actually help, because it would give me deeper technical chops and a larger network. But I don't want to be the person who plans forever and never executes. In the next year, I want to build a prototype of at least one tool and test it with real users.

follow_ups.question: How does mentoring fit into your vision of the future?
follow_ups.response: It's central. I want to always be mentoring. If I lead a research team, I want it to be a place where young Latina scientists feel welcome and supported. I want to grow the Technolatinas program, maybe take it statewide. And if the startup happens, I want to build a company culture that actively recruits from underrepresented communities. I know how much of a difference one encouraging voice can make. Someone did that for me, and I want to keep paying it forward. It's one of the things that gives my career meaning beyond publications and products.

transcript.question_text: What do you value the most in your life?
transcript.response: Family, first and always. The connection to my parents, my brother, my extended family -- that's my foundation. Everything else is built on that. After family, I'd say curiosity. The drive to understand things, to build things, to ask 'what if' -- that's what gets me out of bed. It's what connects the science and the art and even the cooking. I need novelty and learning in my life or I wilt. And community. Not in an abstract sense, but the specific people who show up for me -- Priya, Tom, my mentees, my Discord crew, the neighbors who say hi. I value being part of something larger than myself. I also value honesty. After the divorce, I promised myself I'd be more honest about what I need, what I feel, and what I want. That's been hard but transformative. And beauty. I know that sounds vague, but I mean it practically -- the beauty of a trail at sunrise, of a well-written algorithm, of a painting that captures something words can't. I organize my life around encountering and creating beauty, and I think that's what makes it rich.

follow_ups.question: If you could pass on one piece of wisdom to your mentees, what would it be?
follow_ups.response: That you don't have to choose between being rigorous and being creative. The world will try to put you in a box -- you're a scientist or you're an artist, you're Colombian or you're American, you're practical or you're a dreamer. But the most interesting work and the most fulfilling life happens at the intersections. Stay curious, stay connected to where you came from, and don't let anyone else define what a successful career looks like for you. Build the path that makes sense for your whole self.
